**Stephen Chow Sau-yan**, [S.J.](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 周守仁, born 7 August 1959) is a Hong Kong prelate of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") who has served as [Bishop of Hong Kong](/wiki/List_of_Roman_Catholic_bishops_of_Hong_Kong "List of Roman Catholic bishops of Hong Kong") since 2021. He was the [provincial superior](/wiki/Provincial_superior "Provincial superior") of the Chinese Province of the [Society of Jesus](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") from 2018 to 2021. Chow was elevated to [cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholic_Church) "Cardinal (Catholic Church)") by [Pope Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis") on 30 September 2023.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stephen_Chow_(cardinal)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Chow was born in [British Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong") on 7 August 1959. He studied psychology and philosophy at the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota"), obtaining a [bachelor's degree](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree") and a [master's degree](/wiki/Master%27s_degree "Master's degree") in Educational Psychology.[[1]](#cite_note-HSPO-1)[[2]](#cite_note-O’Connell-2) He joined the [Society of Jesus](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") in 1984, completing his noviceship in 1986. He studied at the [Milltown Institute of Theology and Philosophy](/wiki/Milltown_Institute_of_Theology_and_Philosophy "Milltown Institute of Theology and Philosophy") in [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"), earning a licenciate in philosophy in 1988.[[3]](#cite_note-Diocese_bio-3) He then returned to Hong Kong and taught at [Wah Yan College, Kowloon](/wiki/Wah_Yan_College,_Kowloon "Wah Yan College, Kowloon") for two years. Starting in 1990, he studied theology at the [Holy Spirit Seminary](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_Seminary "Holy Spirit Seminary") in Hong Kong.[[2]](#cite_note-O’Connell-2) On 16 July 1994, Chow was [ordained](/wiki/Holy_orders_in_the_Catholic_Church "Holy orders in the Catholic Church") a priest in the [Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Cathedral_of_the_Immaculate_Conception_(Hong_Kong) "Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception (Hong Kong)") by [John Baptist Wu](/wiki/John_Baptist_Wu "John Baptist Wu"), Bishop of Hong Kong. He undertook postgraduate studies at [Loyola University Chicago](/wiki/Loyola_University_Chicago "Loyola University Chicago"), receiving a master's degree in [organizational development](/wiki/Organizational_development "Organizational development") in 1995.[[1]](#cite_note-HSPO-1)[[2]](#cite_note-O’Connell-2)

## Presbyteral ministry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stephen_Chow_(cardinal)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Presbyteral ministry")]

Chow's first pastoral assignment was at Wah Yan Kowloon and Hong Kong, where he was chaplain, teacher, and school manager from 1996 to 2000.[[2]](#cite_note-O’Connell-2) He began doctoral studies at [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") in 2000 and received his [Doctor of Education](/wiki/Doctor_of_Education "Doctor of Education") degree six years later.[[3]](#cite_note-Diocese_bio-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Lau1-4) His thesis was titled "Understanding Moral Culture in Hong Kong Secondary Schools: Relationships among Moral Norm, Moral Culture, Academic Achievement Motivation, and Empathy".[[3]](#cite_note-Diocese_bio-3)[[5]](#cite_note-5) He made his final vows on 17 April 2007.[[1]](#cite_note-HSPO-1)

Until 2021, Chow served as supervisor of both Wah Yan colleges in Kowloon and [Hong Kong Island](/wiki/Wah_Yan_College,_Hong_Kong "Wah Yan College, Hong Kong").[[1]](#cite_note-HSPO-1)[[2]](#cite_note-O’Connell-2) He was an honorary assistant professor at the [University of Hong Kong](/wiki/University_of_Hong_Kong "University of Hong Kong") from 2007 to 2014, as well as a part-time professor of psychology at Holy Spirit Seminary.[[1]](#cite_note-HSPO-1)[[3]](#cite_note-Diocese_bio-3) He participated at the Jesuits' [36th General Congregation](/wiki/General_Congregation#General_Congregation_36 "General Congregation") in October 2016.[[2]](#cite_note-O’Connell-2) Chow succeeded John Lee Hua as [provincial superior](/wiki/Provincial_superior "Provincial superior") of the Chinese Province of the Society of Jesus on 1 January 2018.[[1]](#cite_note-HSPO-1)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Episcopal ministry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stephen_Chow_(cardinal)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Episcopal ministry")]

Chow was appointed Bishop of Hong Kong on 17 May 2021.[[1]](#cite_note-HSPO-1) The see had been vacant since 3 January 2019, when [Michael Yeung](/wiki/Michael_Yeung "Michael Yeung") died after less than two years in office. His appointment was described as a surprise by the Jesuit magazine *[America](/wiki/America_(magazine) "America (magazine)")* given that he had not been mentioned as one of the likely candidates for the post.[[2]](#cite_note-O’Connell-2) The [Union of Catholic Asian News](/wiki/Union_of_Catholic_Asian_News "Union of Catholic Asian News") had mentioned in January 2019 that two of the candidates were Jesuits, without naming them.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Chow later revealed that he initially declined the bishop's title in December 2020 because he thought the post should go to a member of the [secular clergy](/wiki/Secular_clergy "Secular clergy"). He relented after receiving a handwritten letter from [Pope Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis").[[8]](#cite_note-Lau2-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) He was consecrated on 4 December 2021.[[10]](#cite_note-RTHK1-10)

During his first [Chrism Mass](/wiki/Chrism_Mass "Chrism Mass") as bishop in April 2022, Chow expressed support for [ordained female priests](/wiki/Ordination_of_women_and_the_Catholic_Church "Ordination of women and the Catholic Church"), saying in a homily,

> "I turn to English, just to address our ordained brothers, and I hope one day maybe ordained sister[s] too.”[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In April 2023, Chow visited the [archdiocese of Beijing](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Beijing "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Beijing"). He met with Archbishop [Joseph Li Shan](/wiki/Joseph_Li_Shan "Joseph Li Shan"), visited several churches and the [Zhalan Cemetery](/wiki/Zhalan_Cemetery "Zhalan Cemetery") that holds the tombstone of [Matteo Ricci](/wiki/Matteo_Ricci "Matteo Ricci"). During the visit he told the press:

> "Everyone would like to see their own country do well, no one wants it to do badly. I think it’s everyone’s duty to be patriotic if you're a citizen in Hong Kong or mainland China."[[12]](#cite_note-12)

His was the first Beijing visit by a Hong Kong bishop since 1985.[[13]](#cite_note-13)
After returning to Hong Kong, he elaborated further on his call to patriotism. He said:

> "Like many Hongkongers, I grew up during the colonial era when national sentiment and identity could hardly be part of our consciousness. It can be said that our blood does not carry any love for the nation...It requires extra effort to alter this mentality and it is even more difficult to do so after experiencing the social and political changes over the past decade...I believe both the central and local governments are also aware of this. We really need guidance from the Holy Spirit so we can love our country and our churches at the same time."

He also called for dialogue with the government and that people should help the government improve for the sake of the country.[[14]](#cite_note-14)
In May 2023, Chow commented that he did not believe the Vatican-China deal was 'dead', despite disputes regarding Beijing's unilateral installation of bishops.[[15]](#cite_note-15) He also said that

> "The fate of the church in China is inseparable from that of the country, as all the people of the church are also Chinese."[[16]](#cite_note-16)

On 9 July 2023, Pope Francis announced he plans to make him a cardinal at a consistory scheduled for 30 September.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Chow commented on the appointment:

> I think the appointment as cardinal will strengthen the role of the Catholic Church in Hong Kong as a bridging church, to promote exchanges and interactions between mainland China and the universal Church.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

At the aforementioned consistory, he was made Cardinal-Priest of [San Giovanni Battista de La Salle](/wiki/San_Giovanni_Battista_de_La_Salle "San Giovanni Battista de La Salle").[[19]](#cite_note-19) He was subsequently appointed a member of the [Dicastery for Interreligious Dialogue](/wiki/Dicastery_for_Interreligious_Dialogue "Dicastery for Interreligious Dialogue").[[20]](#cite_note-20) He participated as a [cardinal elector](/wiki/Cardinal_electors_in_the_2025_papal_conclave "Cardinal electors in the 2025 papal conclave") in the [2025 papal conclave](/wiki/2025_papal_conclave "2025 papal conclave") that elected [Pope Leo XIV](/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIV "Pope Leo XIV").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

He has been praised by state media in China as a 'bridge-builder'. [[22]](#cite_note-22)
